standard_description: Form and use comparative and superlative adjectives and adverbs, and choose between them depending on what is to be modified.
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Comparative and superlative adjectives and adverbs
khan_exercises.description: Identify when to use comparative and superlative adjectives and adverbs.
topic_path: Comparative, superlative, intensifiers, and adverbs of degree
Comparative and superlative adjectives and adverbs

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct comparative or superlative!**

>The Cliffs of Moher are \_\_\_\_\_\_ than the White Cliffs of Dover.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
* **Comparative modifiers (-er ending):** We use comparative adjectives/adverbs when one thing is "more/less than" another thing (for example, "the weasel is **bigger** than the mouse.")

* **Superlative modifiers (-est ending):** We use superlative adjectives/adverbs when one thing is "the most/least" of *all other things* (for example, "The elephant is the **biggest** animal at the zoo.")
We're just comparing two things in this sentence, so we should use the comparative modifier **"taller"**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct comparative or superlative!**

>That’s the \_\_\_\_\_\_ three-headed monkey I’ve ever seen!

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
* **Comparative modifiers (-er ending):** We use comparative adjectives/adverbs when one thing is "more/less than" another thing (for example, "the weasel is **bigger** than the mouse.")

* **Superlative modifiers (-est ending):** We use superlative adjectives/adverbs when one thing is "the most/least" of *all other things* (for example, "The elephant is the **biggest** animal at the zoo.")
We're identifying something as the *most* in this sentence, so we should use the superlative modifier **"biggest"**.

khan_exercises.title: Intensifiers and adverbs of degree
khan_exercises.description: Identify and use adverbs of degree. Many of them don't end in "-ly"!
topic_path: Comparative, superlative, intensifiers, and adverbs of degree

items.question_content:
**Identify the appropriate modifier to use in this sentence.**

>You  \_\_\_\_\_\_ touched your dinner.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Intensifiers are adverbs that add emphasis, or accentuate, the meaning of the word they modify. They include words like **"really"** and "**very**".

Adverbs of degree are similar. They answer the question **"how much?"** and include words like **"mostly"** and **"barely"**.
We're modifying the verb "touched", so we should use the adverb **"barely"**.

items.question_content:
**Identify the appropriate modifier to use in this sentence.**

>There are  \_\_\_\_\_\_ any rabbits in this part of the forest.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Intensifiers are adverbs that add emphasis, or accentuate, the meaning of the word they modify. They include words like **"really"** and "**very**".

Adverbs of degree are similar. They answer the question **"how much?"** and include words like **"mostly"** and **"barely"**.
We're modifying the verb "are", so we should use the adverb **"scarcely"**.

Adverbs of degree are similar. They answer the question **"how much?"** and include words like **"mostly"** and **"barely"**.
We're modifying the adjective "quiet", so we should use the adverb **"too"**.

Adverbs of degree are similar. They answer the question **"how much?"** and include words like **"mostly"** and **"barely"**.

We're modifying the verb "arrived", so we should use the adverb **"just"**.

*Note:* the adverb "justly" means to do something in a way that is morally right or fair.
